20q/1. INTRODUCTION
21 Plaintiff Lawrence Less alleges that he slipped and fell on the sidewalk of Leavenworth
22 || Street near McAllister Street in San Francisco. According to the complaint, the three defendants—
23 || University of California, Hastings College of the Law (this demurring defendant), the City and
24 |] County of San Francisco, and Conversion Management Associates, Inc-—owned and/or controlled
25 || the site in question, which plaintiff alleges to have constituted a dangerous condition of public
26 || property. Plaintiff brings a cause of action for general negligence and a cause of action for
27 || premises liability, with counts for negligence and dangerous condition of public property, against
28 || all three defendants.
-|-
MEMORANDUM OR POINTS OF AUTHORITIES IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANT HASTINGS COLLEGE
OF THE LAW’S DEMURRER TO CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF’S COMPLAINT

 
1 Plaintiff correctly alleges that Hastings is a public entity: a University of California law
2 i school. Under California law, Government Code section 835 sets forth the exclusive conditions
3 |] under which a public entity may be liable for a condition of its property. Hastings thus cannot be
4} liable for the allegedly dangerous condition of its property under common-law negligence
5 ]]| principles. Accordingly, plaintiff's cause of action for general negligence and premises-liability
6 || count for negligence fail to state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action against Hastings and
7 || should be dismissed. Moreover, because plaintiff cannot truthfully amend his complaint to support
8 || clatms of common-law negligence or premises liability against Hastings, the demurrer should be
9 || sustained without leave to amend and plaintiff's common-law claims should be dismissed with
10 jl] prejudice.
11} OL. PLEADING ALLEGATIONS
12 Plaintiff filed his complaint on July 1, 2015. (Complaint [Ex. 1 to Request for Judicial
. 13 || Notice filed herewith].) In it, he correctly alleges that Hastings is a public entity: a University of
14] California law school. (Complaint at 2, { 5.a.) Plaintiff brings two causes of action against
15 || Hastings. The first is for general negligence, in which he alleges that he slipped and fell on a metal
16 || plate embedded in the sidewalk on Leavenworth Street near McAllister Street in San Francisco.
17 || (Complaint at 4, | GN-1.) Plaintiff alleges that the plate constituted a dangerous condition of
18 | public property. bid.) He further alleges that Hastings and two additional defendants, the City
19 |] and County of San Francisco and Conversion Management Association, Inc., owned and/or
20 jl] controlled the location in question. (/bid.)
21 Plaintiff's second cause of action is for premises liability, with counts for negligence and
22 || dangerous condition of public property (Hastings does not demur to the latter). (Complaint at 5,
23 |} {| Prem.L-1—Prem.L-4.) In that cause of action, plaintiff also alleges that he slipped on a metal
24 || plate owned, maintained, managed and/or operated by Hastings and the other defendants. (/bid.)
25||/T. ARGUMENT
26 A. Applicable Law
27 A complaint must contain “a statement of the facts constituting the cause of action, in
28 || ordinary and concise language.” (Code Civ. Proc. § 425.10.) The essential elements to be pleaded
0.
MEMORANDUM OR POINTS OF AUTHORITIES IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANT HASTINGS COLLEGE
OF THE LAW’S DEMURRER TO CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF’S COMPLAINT

 
1 i for stating a cause of action are determined by the substantive law. (Brown & Weil, California |
2 || Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial § 6:121 (2015 ed.).) The facts to be pleaded are |
3 |i those on which liability depends—“the facts constituting the cause of action”—which are |
4 | commonly referred to as the “ultimate facts.” (See Doe vy. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th |
5 ff 531, 550.) |
6 Code of Civil Procedure section 430.30(a) provides for demurrers to complaints: |
7 (a) When any ground for objection to a complaint, cross- |
complaint, or answer appears on the face thereof, or from any matter
8 of which the court is required to or may take judicial notice, the
objection on that ground may be taken by demurrer to the pleading.
9
Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10 defines the bases for demurrers:
The party against whom a complaint or cross-complaint has been
11 filed may object, by demurrer or answer as provided in Section
430.30, to the pleading on any one or more of the following grounds:
12
[...]
13
(e) The pleading does not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause
14 of action.
15 | (Emphasis added.)
16 Demutrers for failure to state a cause of action or for lack of subject matter jurisdiction are
17 | commonly referred to as “general” demurrers. (McKenney v. Purepac Pharmaceutical Co. (2008)
18 167 Cal App.4th 72, 77.)
19 B. Government Code section 835 Sets Forth the Exclusive Conditions Under
Which a Public Entity Can Be Liable for a Condition of its Property.
20
Government Code section 815.2 provides, in pertinent part:
21
Except as otherwise provided by statute: |
22
(a) A public entity is not liable for an injury, whether such injury
23 arises out of an act or omission of the public entity or a public
employee or any other person.
25 jj (Emphasis added.) The court explained this principle in Cochran v. Herzog Engraving Co. (1984) |
26 Wt] 155 Cal.App.3d 405, at 409: “[I]n the absence of some constitutional requirement, public entities |
27 || may be liable only if a statute declares them to be liable. ... In short, sovereign immunity is the |
28 || rule in California; governmental liability is limited to exceptions specifically set forth by statute.” |
|| MEMORANDUM OR POINTS OF AUTHORITIES IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANT HASTINGS COLLEGE
OF THE LAW’S DEMURRER TO CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF’S COMPLAINT

 
1 |] (Emphasis in original.) In pleading a case against a public entity, the plaintiff must identify the
2 || statute alleged to have been violated. (Foster v. County of San Luis Obispo (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th |
3 || 668, 672 [citing Searcy v. Hemet Unified School District (1986) 177 Cal.App.3d 792, 798, 802].) :
4 Where a plaintiff alleges negligent conduct, Civil Code section 1714—the State’s general- |
5 | negligence statute—does not provide a sufficient basis for imposing liability on a public entity.
6 || (Eastburn y, Regional Fire Protection Authority (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1175, 1183.) “Otherwise, the
7 || general rule of immunity for public entities would be largely eroded by the routine application of |
8 || general tort principles.” (/bid.) Accordingly, where a plaintiff alleges a dangerous condition of |
9 || public property, Government Code section 835 sets forth the exclusive conditions by which the
10 || public entity can be liable.' (Brown v. Poway Unified School Dist. (1993) 4 Cal.4th 820, 829; |
11 | Nishihama v. City and County of San Francisco (2001) 93 Cal.App.4th 298, 302.)
12 C. A General Demurrer Lies to Plaintiff's Negligence-based Claims Alleging a
Dangerous Condition of Hastings’ Property. :

13 |
As plaintiff correctly alleges, Hastings is a public entity. Both of plaintiff's causes of action |

14 |
against Hastings are based on the allegation that a metal plate owned or controlled by Hastings |

15 :
constituted a dangerous condition. His causes of action against Hastings thus seek to render

16
Hastings liable based on a condition of its property. Under California law, a public entity can only |

17 |
be liable for a condition of its property as set forth in Government Code section 835—to the i

18 |
exclusion of general-negligence principles. (Brown, supra, at 829; Eastburn, supra, at 1183.)

19
20
' Government Code section 835 provides:

21 Except as provided by statute, a public entity is liable for injury
49 caused by a dangerous condition of its property if the plaintiff
establishes that the property was in a dangerous condition at the

73 time of the injury, that the injury was proximately caused by the |
dangerous condition, that the dangerous condition created a

24 reasonably foreseeable risk of the kind of injury which was
incurred, and that either:

. 25 (a) A negligent or wrongful act or omission of an employee
of the public entity within the scope of his employment created the |

26 dangerous condition; or
7 (b) The public entity had actual or constructive notice of |
the dangerous condition under Section 835.2 a sufficient time prior

28 to the injury to have taken measures to protect against the
dangerous condition. |

-4-

MEMORANDUM OR POINTS OF AUTHORITIES IN SUPPORT OF DEFENDANT HASTINGS COLLEGE

OF THE LAW’S DEMURRER TO CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF’S COMPLAINT

 
1 i Plaintiff's cause of action for general negligence and premise-liability count for negligence thus :
2 | fail to state facts sufficient to constitute causes of action against Hastings. Accordingly, a general |
3 || demurrer lies to these claims. Moreover, because plaintiff has already—and correctly—alleged
4 || that Hastings is a public entity and that he was injured due to a condition of its property, he cannot
5 || truthfully amend his complaint to state a claim for general negligence or negligence-based |
6 || premises liability against Hastings. These claims should thus be dismissed with prejudice.
7\|IV. CONCLUSION
8 For the foregoing reasons, Hastings respectfully requests that the court sustain its demurrer |
9 || and dismiss plaintiff's cause of action for general negligence and premises-liability count for |
10 || negligence with prejudice. |
